chunk_summary: Explores adolescent safeguards in nutrigenomics, emphasizing assent, privacy, and disordered eating risks, with guidelines for ethical genetic testing and data governance in pediatric care.
learning_objectives: Understand the ethical considerations for adolescent assent in genetic testing.
Identify risks of disordered eating linked to nutrigenomic results and restrictive diets.
Learn privacy and data governance frameworks relevant to pediatric genetic testing in India.
Apply actionable guidelines for genetic testing in adolescents, including screening and counseling.
key_facts: Adolescents are vulnerable to genetic risk framing, which can worsen disordered eating or trigger stigma.
AAP/ACMG discourage direct-to-consumer (DTC) genetic testing in children due to weak oversight and interpretation.
The Indian DPDP Act (2023) establishes a national framework for processing digital personal data, including genetic reports.
Developmentally appropriate assent and parental consent are required for adolescent genetic testing.
Screening for disordered eating risk is essential before endorsing restriction-based diet changes.
reference_quote: AAP/ACMG discourage DTC genetic testing in children partly because oversight and interpretation are weak, but digital data handling adds another risk layer.
document_key_topics: Adolescent assent and ethical safeguards in genetic testing
Disordered eating risks linked to nutrigenomics
Privacy and data governance in pediatric genetic testing
